speaker: interviewer
text: OK Sonya, you were describing to me what happened when you arrived at Auschwitz and when you were assigned to the barracks. Describe to me what you saw again.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were assigned to the barracks, they gave us clothes because we were naked till then. They gave us-- so, you know, they took you into a room. And there was piles and piles of laying there. And you just picked, you know, first best thing, and you put it on. No panties, no, no underwear, just that dress. And we were put into the barracks. And I was in Auschwitz for about 11 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do those 11 days while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, oh, excuse me. I forgot. In Auschwitz, we weren't-- we were on the floor. We were laying on the floor. I forgot. In Auschwitz, we were on the floor. We were laying one next to the other on the floor like sardines in a can. If-- you know, if I turn over, the other person had to turn over too. And this is how we were laying there. And twice a day, they would give us some-- they said soup, but it was like water. And we did nothing there. We were just laying there day and night. And this-- you know, there were-- in the morning, we had to wake up 5 o'clock in the morning. And they would count us. And we had to stay, let's say, a distance-- like, five in a row, you know, this way and God knows how far this way. And we would-- could stay sometimes for two hours, sometimes three hours. And they were counting us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they made us go back to the barracks. And we were just sitting around there. There was no-- you can only-- they had, let's say in the morning, you can-- they had like, let's say, like, not a sink, but like a long-- made from wood. And they had, like, faucets, different faucets, one next to the other, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you just have to go after. You stayed there outside for all this time. Then you had to go and wash yourself, shower. Not a shower. It was just wash yourself, you know, under this thing. You had to wash your head, your whole body. And there was no soap-- just with the water. And then you put back this dress. You didn't even dry yourself. And there was certain times that you could go to the-- excuse me-- to the toilet. And this was like holes in the-- on a site someplace with very little privacy. It was just one next to the other, and there was just like a wall. But from-- this is how-- this were our toilets. And you could only go a certain time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there for 11 days or 12 days. I don't remember exactly. And all of a sudden, they made us go up, And one, when we sent for after they counted us, they told us to, you know, make rows over there, you know? And they yelled and screamed and kicked you and hit you. And it was just horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you ran for your life. They assessed you with their rifle butts and that the women screamed. It was just like chaos. And you wound up in these rows. And I was among them. And we didn't know where we're going. We thought maybe they're taking us to, you know-- and the first night that we got there in Auschwitz and the barracks had like, like a chimney in the middle of the-- and there was this-- she was like a kapo or somebody. And she would scream and yell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she showed us what's going on outside. You see the chimneys? You see the smoke coming out? These are your parents. They're being burned there. And you know, and, and we just couldn't believe it. The one that-- the ones that went on the other side? They're burning them now over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- you know, we just-- we were just numb to the whole thing. You were just-- you didn't know where you were. You didn't know your name. You didn't know what you look like. Because you didn't know what you looked like. You could see somebody else. You couldn't see myself because there was no mirror. There was no window. There was no-- you didn't see what you looked like. It was just horrendous. You could-- you know, it was unbelievable. We were reduced to worse than animals because you had nothing. You had no hair. You had no clothes. You had nothing. And they were just cussing you and calling names and beating you. Anyway, we got from-- I was in this row already. And then they took me to over there and they put us on a train again. And we didn't know where we're going. And we wound up in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was about a day and a night that we traveled. I really don't remember exactly, Carol. And we wound up in Stutthof. And Stutthof, that time, was the-- you know, this by then, it was already, you know, getting cold. And the same thing-- we were just dressed in this one little dress. And we stayed-- were in Stutthof where they took us off those trains. And again, they chased us and pushed us into the rows. And we walked and walked and walked until we came to Stutthof. We came-- we didn't know where we are at the time, you know. But we got to this place. And at that time where there was just, you know. At that time, there were these bridges. And there were three rows. And we were just-- there was-- if there were just two, they put one in. And there were new ones that-- so we wound up four in one. And I was on the third floor with one-- you know, with three other girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, we were frozen, cold, you know? And we just got close up to their bodies, to the girls, and covered myself with the blanket. And I was laying there maybe for I don't know how long until I warmed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, I felt like a million bugs are biting me. And when daylight broke, I saw the blanket and my dress was full of lice and bugs. It was just-- you know, and my body was just bitten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just tried to, you know, you know, kind of push it away, rub it off my body. It was all over my body. And I got off from the thing, you know? And I was just standing there. I didn't want to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was the thing in Stutthof. And I was there. And after, you know, we were there, I don't remember-- maybe two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Practically nothing. Just in the morning-- in the morning-- oh yes, I got very sick there. In the morning, they would count us, you know, take it out at dawn. And we had to stand. It was freezing cold by then. And in the snow-- you had to stay barefoot in the snow. And we have to stand like this-- you know, this far apart from each other because so there wouldn't be-- you couldn't get warm from another body. And they counted us, and we would stay there for hours and hours. And then we would have to go and wash, you know, in the cold water. And then we would go back. And about that time already, we had to like keep our barracks-- you know, we had to wash the floors. We have to go for the soup, you know, and things like this. And by that time, after two months, there was so many people started dying. And everybody-- typhoid fever. And I-- you know, and I got sick too. And I was on the third-- on the third thing. And at that time, they brought us a little piece of bread, you know, and a little water. If you couldn't go down, you didn't get it. So we were just laying there and, you know, and then after a while, one of the girls died. They came in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: They looked what's going on. And one of the girls died and they just pushed her down, you know, like-- and when she fell down it made such a tug, you now? The next night, another one died. The third night, [INAUDIBLE]. And I was so sick and I had such a high temperature. My lips were burning. It was just, you know, I couldn't feel anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- when they threw the third girl down, I somehow said to myself, I don't want to be thrown down like this. I'm going to go down. After I die, I don't want, want to be thrown down like that. And I just-- I don't know how. I got down, and I just sat down at the wall. There was-- let's say the barracks. The [INAUDIBLE] were until here. And there was a wall, let's say, like over there. I just-- I don't know how I got there. I just sat at the wall. And other girls, you know, when they walked by with a little water in their pot or whatever, they would wash my face. They would get me to drink some water. And slowly-- and they would bring me some soup because I couldn't help myself anymore. And slowly, slowly, I got stronger. And, and somehow, I got out of it. I don't know how, but I got out of it. And we were there. Let's see, when I got better, they took us to-- a whole bunch of us. They picked out always the ones that were looking better and still able to work. And they took me-- and they took us to-- again, we had to stand in line. And we didn't know where we're going, you know. And sometimes, they would, like, make-- let's say they would count, let's say, so many rows, you know-- let's say 200 or whatever. And I would be, let's say, the last one. And they would ask us to go. They took them out and-- I don't know where they took them. And they were the last one. Then the rest could go back to the barracks. This went down for all the time. Anyway, at that time, they took us to Stutthof-- no. From-- to this-- to this camp where we worked. So I don't know how long it took, but it took for-- it wasn't really that far. And they took us to work there. And over there was already a little better. And we had to go. And they gave us clothes. They gave us just asleep two in a bed. And over there was one German Jewish woman that she was like the head of that-- of the barrack that I was in. And she was really very nice to me. And we went to work every morning, and we came home at night. And we worked on this, this highway that was supposed to go from Berlin to Moscow. So we worked at a certain portion of this highway with picks, iron picks and shovels. And if somebody couldn't-- if you didn't work fast enough, they were standing over you and hitting you. And if you couldn't work fast enough, they shot you right then and there. So every day we came out, we came back less. Every time, we came back less. And it came to the point where you didn't even look who it was that they shot. You just wanted to walk by and get back to your barrack. And we got back to the barracks, over there was already a little better. This woman, she gave us pieces of material. And I remember I made myself a skirt. I make myself a brassiere. I made-- you know, little things that she showed us how, this woman. And it was already-- it was-- this was-- you know, and we got much better-- I mean, food to sustain us. And I was there maybe four months. And while I was there, there was one woman there that she was the only person that I knew. And she was my-- and I found her there. It was my aunt's-- a close friend of my aunt's. And so, you know, when I saw her, like I was a messiah. You know, and she-- you know, we just stuck together all the time. And then she got sick. And they send her back to Stutthof. And I was still on this working camp. After a while, we were there I don't know how many months. And we worked. And then all of a sudden, this-- we finished a certain portion that we were supposed to do. And they sent us back to Stutthof. And in Stutthof again was the same thing. Every day, they made-- and at that time, when I came back to Stutthof, I-- there was-- they put them together on a bed with four girls-- with three girls. And one, somehow in my mind, she looked like my sister to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know-- and we became so close, you know? I would-- you know, we would do everything together. And we would scrub the floors together. We would do things together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would go pick up soup together, everything together until they send me out from Auschwitz-- from Stutthof. And they sent me to-- and I was trying to get her too. But we didn't know where we were going. And I tried to go back to, to for her to get her. And they beat me so much over my head that my head was bleeding. And you know, and I, I kind of like I was blacked out. I didn't know what was going on. And they just you know, pushed us and pushed us. And they took us to a place and we stayed there. I don't know how long we stayed there on an empty lot outside. And at that time was maybe February ready. And we would just there in that place, and we were laying-- sitting there. And they put us in lorries-- you know, like when you see sometimes in the coal mines, lorries like this. They put us in these lorries-- you know, four or five, you know, pushed in. And we went with this open little, you know, like a train. And we took us to this camp. We wouldn't know where we-- you know, where we-- where we were, the name of it, or whatever. And we were there. And they took us there. And over there was about, let me see, maybe 400 women. And this was a camp that was just for-- there was Russians and French and no Jews there, just, just foreigners. Like, I know it was a-- because wherever we went, we heard all kinds of languages. Poles-- and, you know, and we worked there. And at that time already, it was almost daily in this place. It was near Danzig and Putzig. And there were daily bombings. And we saw like horses being killed, people being killed. You know, the Germans were fighting the Russians-- constant bombing. And many times, you know, there was-- you ran by here. And there was a hole here that people died. It was just horrendous. And after a while, the Germans decided they're going to take us out from there. And, you know, and they were talking. And there was burning all over. Wherever you looked, you saw fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was on fire. And we were standing in line with some, some people. And they said that-- where are they taking us? Where are they taking us? And they were saying that they're taking us to the ocean, to the Baltic Sea, and shooting, and shooting us, which was-- that's what turned out to be. And by that time, I was so tired and I was so disgusted. And I said, I'm not going to this woman next to me. I said, I'm not going. She said, what do you mean you're not going? What are you going to do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't know. But I'm not going. Let them shoot me here. I don't want to go anymore. I don't have the strength. I don't have the will. And just don't care because they're going to shoot us someplace anyway. They're not going to let us live anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So while we were marching out of this thing, there was-- I saw like a little outhouse. And I had a little pot that you drink water from. So I-- everybody just marched out. And I just went into this little-- in the outhouse, I put it-- I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: I put the pot outside the outhouse. And I went into the outhouse. And I was laying there. Everybody marched out from the thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I already-- you know, sitting there and it was so-- it was death quiet. And I already regretted what I did. I thought to myself, you know, where am I?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where we are. I don't know what's going to happen to me now. And I already regret what I did. And I was-- and all of a sudden, about toward dawn-- toward the evening, you know, the SS goes by. And they're looking for people. And he kicks this little can, whatever it was, and did not-- and I was sitting there frozen. I thought he's going to open the door. He's going to shoot me right there. And he walked away. And I was just sitting there. And then it got-- there was a hospital there with sick people. And I was just sitting there in the thing. And I didn't know what I'm going to do, where I am, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, you know, it was-- it got dark. And all of a sudden, I hear people. I see people that are dragging other people on stretchers from the hospital and they're taking-- because it was constant bombings. And they're taking them into bunkers, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, I hear somebody walk by me, by the thing. And I hear Polish and then Russian and then other languages. Because I was [INAUDIBLE] with the Poles. And I hear other languages. And then-- and it was toward dawn. I open the door. And I saw where they're taking those people. And there were people hidden in the hospital at that time when I went out from there and we went to these bunkers. And the bunkers were a lot of provisions that the Germans had. There were bunkers that they were hidden. And we were in the bunker for about a week till the-- till the Russians came to liberate us. But while we were there, there were two other Jewish girls from Lodz. And we kind of stuck together. And the Russians came to liberate us. In the meantime, this whole week, if somebody went out to get some water, if somebody went out [INAUDIBLE], we were just looking what's going on. And people are constantly got killed when they went out of the bunkers. It was just like raining bombs. And but it was lucky that there was food-- you know, cans and things in the thing. And there were-- this camp were, like, some Poles, mostly men. Not mostly-- all men. Poles, Russians, French were in there. And they took out all the sick people from the-- from the hospitals. And there were no Jews. There were other-- because there were no Jews in that camp. And they took them all down into the-- into the-- into the bunkers. And then all of a sudden, we saw the Russians. The Russians came and they liberated us. And it was also chaos. We had to hide from them. They were-- they were raping the girls. It was terrible. And we just wanted to get out of there. And there were no-- it was no transportation. There was no nothing. There was only these trains going with the people from-- the wounded that they were taking back from the front and the Russian and German prisoners of war and mostly Russians. And we, we stayed there for about a week after the liberation. And you know, we-- some, they were sick. A lot of them were sick. And they were taken to hospitals. And the other ones were just left to fend for themselves. And we found one Russian, you know, higher up. And he, you know, brought us clothes and food. And the clothes were, like, I came home to Lodz in a men's coat. Whatever they gave you-- whatever they had, they gave you. And it took about a week till we came, till we were sitting not in the trains, but between, you know, when the trains have like things in between. That's where we were sitting with three girls. And we went back to Lodz. And it took about a week until we got back to Lodz. But what we suffered on the way from the-- from the Russian-- you know, from the Russian soldiers-- they were constantly after us. They were constantly wanting to-- it was just-- if we got out not raped, it was just a miracle. And the week that we were there, there were older women. They were sisters, two sisters, and one-- and a mother. Let's say they told that they want-- the Russians, they want 10 girls for the night. And they came, and they just took 10 girls. If you were-- we were hiding in closets and whatever we could to avoid them. It was the most horrible thing to go through. And there were mothers that went in order for their-- to spare their daughters or sisters. Because one was with us. She said, I was married anyway. If my husband survived, he wouldn't know difference if I'm a-- if I was raped or not in order to save us-- her sister, and me. And then on the train, it was constant, you know, running and fighting. You know, when this train stopped for a minute, you had to run. You have to-- not to stand in one place because you were scared. All you heard is Russian. And I really don't know how we made it there. And we got into Lodz. It was the last day of Passover. And there were some-- you know, a lot of people in the streets in Lodz at that time. Because 800 people-- you know, at that time, a lot of people were already liberated. And Lodz was liberated and all that. So there was a lot of people already, Jewish people, in Lodz. And one of the-- one of the women recognized somebody. We went to-- we asked where the Jewish committee is. And there were a lot of men there. And she recognized one of the men. And she started talking to him. And she told him that we just came back, you know. And we looked-- I can't begin to tell you how we looked. Not like human beings-- worse than animals. With the, you know-- from the smoke, from the-- from the train, and the dirt. And we didn't have a bath for all this time. Anyway, he took us home to his house and threw away all the clothes that we had on. We took a bath, you know. And he put us in a bedroom, you know, to go to sleep, to rest. And while I was there, I couldn't sleep. And I heard some people came in. And I heard a voice. And I-- this-- I thought this sounds like somebody that I know. And I got off to bed. And I opened the door a crack. And I saw my friends. At that time, it wasn't her husband. It was her friend that she lived with in the ghetto. It turns out that he was among the 800 that stayed in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took the blanket, you know, because I didn't have clothes. They gave us just a nightgown, you know. And I opened the door and I said, Aaron? And he saw me, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just like I saw God. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And he took me home to his house. You know, he went home and he took clothes from his wife-- panties and brassiere that I didn't have for God knows how long-- and clothes. Because we looked like sisters. Everybody afterward thought that we were sisters. Only she was always very skinny. And she brought me clothes and everything. And he took me home to his house.
